abstract: While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable success in single-label image classification, adapting them for multi-label imagery remains a significant challenge. This difficulty stems primarily from the complexity of varied object layouts and a lack of sufficiently annotated multi-label training data. In this paper, we introduce Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP), a flexible deep learning architecture designed to overcome these hurdles. The HCP framework processes an arbitrary number of object segment hypotheses through a shared CNN; the resulting outputs are then consolidated via max pooling to generate final multi-label predictions. Key advantages of the HCP architecture include its ability to train without ground-truth bounding box annotations and its inherent robustness to noisy or redundant hypotheses. Furthermore, the shared CNN component is highly adaptable and can be effectively pre-trained on massive single-label datasets like ImageNet. Our experimental evaluations on the Pascal VOC 2007 and VOC 2012 benchmarks demonstrate that HCP significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods. Notably, HCP achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 90.5% on VOC 2012, which increases to 93.2% when fused with complementary hand-crafted features.
